---
title: "Data Analysis in Political Science"
subtitle: "PLSC 296"
author: "Justin Leinaweaver"
institute: "Drury University"
date: "Fall 2021"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---


```{r, echo = FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
library(tidyverse)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: center, middle, slidegreen

.size40[**PLSC296: Data Analysis in Political Science**]

.size60[
If you arrange all the students in our class by height, who represents the exact middle of the distribution?
]

???

*opening slide and exercise as students come in*

If you arrange all the students in our class by height, who represents the exact middle of the distribution?

Make sure to introduce yourselves to each other!

* Let them work on this on their own *

OK, HOW DID WE DO? WHO REPRESENTS THE EXACT MIDDLE?

* Ask a Volunteer *
HOW TALL ARE YOU TO THE INCH?

TO THE TENTH OF AN INCH?

TO THE HUNDREDTHS OF AN INCH?

TO THE THOUSANDTHS OF AN INCH?

WHY DID YOU STOP?

HOW CERTAIN ARE YOU IN YOUR ANSWER? PLEASE QUANTIFY YOUR UNCERTAINTY: E.G. + OR - A HALF INCH, A QUARTER INCH, ETC.


CLASS: WHAT ARE THE SPECIFIC SOURCES OF YOUR UNCERTAINTY IN OUR MEASUREMENTS? 


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_subject.jpg')
background-size: 60%
background-position: right

.left[.size60[

+ **Subject**

+ Technique

+ Tool

+ Validation
]]

???

Subject
- People are all different shapes, sizes, hairstyles, bone structures


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_technique.jpg')
background-size: 50%
background-position: right

.left[.size60[

+ Subject

+ **Technique**

+ Tool

+ Validation
]]

???

Technique
- Are you sure you were standing the right way?
- The same as everybody else?
- Person who did it knew what they were doing?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_rulers.jpg')
background-size: 60%
background-position: right

.left[.size60[

+ Subject

+ Technique

+ **Tool**

+ Validation
]]

???

Tool
- Tool used?
- That the equipment was used as defined by the manufacturer?
- That the manufacturer didn't make any big mistakes in production?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-Height_calibration.jpg')
background-size: 60%
background-position: right

.left[.size60[

+ Subject

+ Technique

+ Tool

+ **Validation**
]]

???

Validation
- That the manufacturer evaluated and calibrated their design to an "official" measure?
- That the calibration was done correctly?
- That the "official" measure was used correctly and isn't shedding atoms too fast? and on and on.

So, clearly, there are many, many sources of uncertainty.

WHAT DOES THAT MEAN FOR US IN THE END? DO WE ACTUALLY KNOW HOW TALL YOU ARE? WHY OR WHY NOT? 


---

class: center, middle, slideblue

.size60[If you arrange all the students in our class by height, who represents the exact middle of the distribution?]

???

Go back to the question.

IS OUR MEASURE PRECISE ENOUGH TO ANSWER THIS QUESTION? WHY OR WHY NOT?

Even if we aren't using the "best" ruler in the "right" way, as long as we measure consistently across our sample in the room we can answer the question accurately!

Ultimately, even if we all admit we don't know the "truth," e.g. how tall we are, we know enough to answer our beginning question.

And THAT'S the key.

Even a cheap wooden 12" ruler will let us order the class by height! That's the point.



---

class: center, middle, slideblue

.size60[ **ALL measures are uncertain.** ]

<br>
<br>
<br>

.size60[The key is to understand the uncertainty well enough so you can answer a question.]

???

- ALL measures are uncertain.

- The key is to understand the uncertainty well enough so you can answer a question.

So, what is our aim in this class?

In short, it is to help you learn to analyze and think critically about uncertainty.


---

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', fig.dim=c(7,4), out.width='100%'}
## , , fig.width = 6
d <- tibble(
    x = 0:15,
    y = c(0, 18, 18, 18, 18:9, NA, NA),
    y1 = c(0, 18, 18, 18, 18:9, 11, 15),
    y2 = c(0, 18, 18, 18, 18:9, 7, 3)    
)


d %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y)) +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = "Your expertise in the subject",
         y = "Your belief the conclusion is nonsense") +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 20)) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 7, 15), labels = c("None", "", "Tons")) +
    scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 9, 19), labels = c("0%", "50%", "100%"))
```

???

So, what is our aim in this class?

In short, it is to help you learn to analyze and think critically about uncertainty.

SLIDE

Here's one way I like to think about our class.

On the y-axis I can visualize your "belief a scientific conclusion is nonsense".

On the x-axis your level of expertise in a given field.


---

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', fig.dim=c(7,4), out.width='100%'}
d <- tibble(
    x = 0:15,
    y = c(0, 18, 18, 18, 18:9, NA, NA),
    y1 = c(0, 18, 18, 18, 18:9, 11, 15),
    y2 = c(0, 18, 18, 18, 18:9, 7, 3)    
)


p1 <- d %>%
    ggplot(aes(y = y)) +
    geom_line(aes(x = x), size = 1.3) +
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = "Your expertise in the subject",
         y = "Your belief the conclusion is nonsense") +
    coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 20)) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 7, 15), labels = c("None", "", "Tons")) +
    scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 9, 19), labels = c("0%", "50%", "100%"))

p1
```


---

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', fig.dim=c(7,4), out.width='100%'}
p1 +
    annotate("text", x = 5, y = 0, label = "Being honest with ourselves", size = 4, color = "blue") +
    annotate("segment", x = 1.75, xend = .25, y = 0, yend = 0, color = "blue", size = .75, arrow = arrow())

```

???

Here's my stab at the approximate relationship.

SLIDE

If we're being truly honest with ourselves we'd admit we're at zero for almost everything in the world.

Our democracy would be much healthier if human beings could simply accept this fact.



---

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', fig.dim=c(7,4), out.width='100%'}
p2 <- p1 +
    annotate("segment", x = 1, xend = 4, y = 18, yend = 18, color = "red", size = 1.5) +
    annotate("segment", x = 2.5, xend = 2.5, y = 14, yend = 17.5, color = "red", size = .75, arrow = arrow()) +
    annotate("text", x = 2.8, y = 12, label = "Hot Takes\nLive Here", size = 4, color = "red")

p2
```

???

Here's the cable news portion of our line plot.

Hot takes are frequently born from having "some" knowledge about a subject.

This semester we will examine data projects that all give answers or measurements for REALLY hard questions.

How free are we?

What is terrorism?

Is American democracy in danger of collapsing?

The easy part will be making a list of the many, many ways they could be wrong. 


---

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', fig.dim=c(7,4), out.width='100%'}
p3 <- p2 +
    annotate("segment", x = 4, xend = 13, y = 18, yend = 9, color = "green4", size = 1.5) +
    annotate("segment", x = 6, xend = 11, y = 14.5, yend = 9.5, arrow = arrow(), color = "green4") +
    annotate("text", x = 9, y = 14.5, label = "Goal 1", angle = -25, size = 4, color = "green4")

p3
```

???

Our first job this semester is to dig into the measures and procedures to better understand how we should and shouldn't use the data to answer these questions.

This is REALLY hard.

Every measure means making choices and most of the time those choices will be problematic.

The real skill is learning to think like the scientists whose work you are analyzing.

Why did they make the choices they did and how do those choices impact what we can use the data to do?



---

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.retina=3, fig.align = 'center', fig.dim=c(7,4), out.width='100%'}
p3 +
    geom_ribbon(aes(x = x, ymin = y1, ymax = y2), alpha = .7, fill = "green3") +
    annotate("text", x = 14, y = 9, label = "Goal 2", color = "white")

```

???

Once we understand the choices made by these researchers our second goal is to draw conclusions about them.

How can we use this data?

How could we improve this data?

QUESTIONS ON MY GOALS FOR YOU THIS SEMESTER?

- Remember, the science in the process of generating knowledge is the method, not the answer.

- Scientific knowledge means understanding the uncertainty in an answer.

- A "scientific" answer shows its work and makes clear what you can and can't do with that piece of information.

- Answers that don't change given data and don't consider uncertainty aren't worth the paper they probably aren't written on.



---

.size30[**LO1: Methods Knowledge**]

.size30[Students should demonstrate an ability to use statistical software (e.g. Excel, R, SPSS, etc) to organize, explore and visualize data in order to answer important political science questions.]

<br>

--

.size30[**LO2: Critical Thinking & Problem Solving Skills**]

.size30[Students should demonstrate an understanding of how research design impacts our confidence in the claims made by researchers, politicians and the media.]

<br>

--

.size30[**LO3: Communication Skills**]

.size30[Students should demonstrate effective written and data visualization skills.]


---

class: slideblue

.size40[**The Big Idea**]

<br>

.size30[
To answer political science questions with data you will develop skills in:

- **Evaluating** data for validity/reliability

- **Cleaning/Preparing** the data

- **Describing** the variation in the data

- **Analyzing** the data to answer questions and test models of politics

- **Reporting** on your findings using clear writing and visualizations
]


---

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_1-learn_code.jpg")
```

???

This semester we learn to code!

1. Learning to code is like learning the language of data

- No matter what career you end up pursuing, you will benefit from being literate in this language.



2. Learning to code means learning to break down complicated tasks into simple steps.

- You'll be amazed by how much you can accomplish once you learn some very basic code.

- This approach to problem-solving will likely also inform how you attack problems in other areas of your life.



3. We are training you to be scientists and scientific arguments require transparency and replicatability.

- Doing analyses in code means having a written record of every step you took in your process

- Other scientists can check your work or build on top of it!



---

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_1-excel_dangerous.png")
```

???

Doing data analyses in Excel is incredibly dangerous

- No record of what you changed,

- Super easy to accidentally edit cells without meaning to,




---

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_1-excel_danger2.png")
```

???

The auto formatting features may change your data (e.g. it thinks you entered a date!)



---

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_1-companies_using_R.png")
```



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-NYT_Example.png')
background-size: 80%
background-position: center

???

NYT, WSJ, the Economist all use R to create dynamic visualizations

There are tons of coding languages, why are we learning R?

1. It is free,

2. cross-platform, and

3. cutting-edge.


---

class: middle, center

.size40[**You will make this in Week 13!**]

<br>
<br>

```{r, fig.retina=3, fig.align='center', fig.asp=0.4, out.width='100%', fig.width=8, cache=TRUE, echo=FALSE, eval=TRUE}
library(gganimate)
library(gapminder)

gapminder |>
  filter(continent != "Oceania") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, size = pop, color = country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  facet_wrap(~ continent, ncol = 4) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(3, 13)) +
  transition_time(year) + #<<
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', x = "GDP per capita (log 10)", y = "Life Expectancy") 
```

???

You'll make this exact plot in week 13.



---

class: slideblue

.size40[**The Big Idea**]

<br>

.size30[
To answer political science questions with data you will develop skills in:

- **Evaluating** data for validity/reliability

- **Cleaning/Preparing** the data

- **Describing** the variation in the data

- **Analyzing** the data to answer questions and test models of politics

- **Reporting** on your findings using clear writing and visualizations
]

???

Back to the big idea

Hands on data analysis class using R

Software is on all lab computers and that's where you should start working.

HOWEVER, I will strongly encourage you to get comfortable installing, setting up and using R on your personal computer.

I REALLY want you to take all of these skills with you!


---

class: middle

.size60[**Course Grade**]

<br>

.size30[
||Proportion|
|:-----|:----:|
|Participation|50%|
|Assignments|50%|
|||
|Examples:||
|- Analyze a data project: Regime type||
|- Analyze a data project: International outcome||
|- Complete a research report||
|- Present a Research Finding||

]

???

Course Grade

Heavy on the participation.

You guys will steer us this semester. You pick the projects and I'll keep you moving forward.



---

class: middle, slideblue

.size60[**Participation**]

.size40[
1. Get to class on time,

2. Have the materials you need to be productive,

3. Do the work in class, and

4. Save your work (annotated code and data).
]



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-office_space.jpg')
background-size: 68%
background-position: center

???

Excused absence coming up?

Complete a make-up assignment to earn your participation.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01_1-coyote.jpg')
background-size: 84%
class: center, bottom

???

The Attendance Cliff



---

class: middle

.pull-left[ 

```{r, echo = FALSE, fig.align = 'center', out.width = '75%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/01_1-naked_stats.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

.size50[**Online Resources**]

.size30[
+ Healy (2019) https://socviz.co/

+ Chang (2021) https://r-graphics.org/

+ Long and Teetor (2019) https://rc2e.com/

+ Wickham (2017) https://r4ds.had.co.nz/

]]


???

Only one book to buy and it should be like $10.

The rest are available online.

Tons of R resources online, feel free to use them!



---

class: middle, slidepurple

.size60[**For Thursday**]

.size50[
1. Install R and RStudio on your computer (bring your laptop to class), 

2. Review the options for our first data project (be ready to vote)

3. Bring snacks!
]
